item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: o_O. I don't care if you hate emoticons, just o_O.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm curious what would happen if it were cross tested with SCP 676. Clearly that would top the list of "REALLY BAD IDEAS DO NOT TRY", but tossing a fountain full of matter destroying liquid made of the souls of dead children into a pool of matter destroying liquid that produces normal water would certainly be interesting. What could possibly go wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh…wow. Is this that bubble that pops you idea I saw long ago? You have done it perfectly. +1 And probably a new favorite of mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read this in the sandbox, so glad its up.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Natural bubbles have the same pressure inside as the atmosphere when they were formed. Assuming 1518-1 are similar in that respect, high-pressure would have no effect.

Plasma is an interesting idea. Given that the bubbles react differently to solid, liquid, and gas (mutual annihilation, annihilation plus growth, and no reaction; respectively) the fourth state of matter could provoke nearly anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why build a 5 million dollar containment system when you can build a $500k one that requires not much more maintenance than a foam gun and a full-time D-Class under the supervision of a technical team, presumably one which oversees several SCPs at once?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reddish limestones exist they are just rather rare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If this doesn't do damage to gasses, what happens when the fountain would be put under high pressure? Or isolated by a screen of plasma in a magnetic suspension?

The SCP object is very nice though. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the mainlist, evil bubble fountain! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the liquid doesn't evaporate, it probably doesn't freeze either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The world will then implode upon itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if you froze it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the song +1. However, I would suggest rewriting the containment section try to vary the sentence structure, instead of having "The _ is" start every sentence.

Also, keep this away from 109

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're going to deal with a sensitive topic, I think you should strive to do something worthy of invoking it, not just try "not to offend anyone." As it is you aren't offensive, just ineffective, meaning you're letting the horror of the real life event do the heavy lifting which just underscores the shortcomings of that part of the text. Really I'm not sure you need the diary of the soldier at all; "for clarity" translation or not, you aren't doing justice to the event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand completely with the desire to not offend, but the topic itself is offensive; and, as DrNietzsche pointed out (Very interesting, by the way), the character seems too disinterested and distant from what he is doing, and not in a "I'm coping and rationalizing" sort of way. I think that desire to not offend, which is a goal that I certainly understand, effected the realism of the journals to me. That, to me, hurts your goal of making the journals seem disturbing- in the end, the disturbing comes from the content, not the tone of the writer.

That said, I hadn't considered the translation point. I'll have to think about that. I can speak a little bit of Bosnian, so maybe I'll grab my text and dictionary and work on the verisimilitude of the translation, for my own benefit, but as far as it goes for the story it could convince me to overlook my own problems.

Also, no need to apologize- I didn't think it offensive, but that something that was already good could be improved for a little bit more emotional resonance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure. I've not read much from the Yugoslav conflict, but I've got shelves of various published dairies from the Einsatzkommando. That's basically what the diary was from, a 1990s/2000s Serbian Einsatzkommando unit, carrying out the same tasks with simply a different target. If anything, he seems a bit too…not unenthusiastic, but he seems too disinterested. One does not join the spiritual successor of the largest use of industrialized murder without genuinely believing in the cause. Granted, Himmler did get nauseous and such at executions, so I suppose this guy could be similar, albeit of infinitely smaller rank.

As for the SCP itself, +1. My dad was involved with the KFOR, so this brings forth an interesting amount of feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The journal entries first and foremost were designed specifically to avoid offending anyone, since this is such a sensitive topic. In addition, this is "for clarity" translation, so some of the edge to the dialogue is lost. Furthermore, I intended on making the genocide seem like a cheap and trivial issue in the journal, to emphasize the disturbing mindset of those engaged in ethnic cleansing.

I have never been to Bosnia, so I apologize for any shortcomings in tone or realism; one of the reasons I did this scip is because I wrote a report on ethnic cleansing in Bosnia when I was much younger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've studied and lived in Bosnia, so I was very interested to see a Bosnian SCP, but the journal entries really rubbed me the wrong way. They don't feel realistic to me at all, and it makes me agree with what Vezaz said above- you really treated ethnic cleansing too… distantly, so that it just feels a little cheap to invoke it. I feel like it could work here, but it needs to be more real to the reader.

So, I spent some time thinking about them and how I would write them, but I'm not sure I really feel comfortable posting my thoughts on making the writings of someone engaged in genocidal ethnic cleansing more realistic. Author, if you are interested in revising the journal entries and want my input, let me know and I'd be happy to help. If you don't think it matters or isn't worth it, fair enough.

Overall, I like most of it, but I feel like the journal entries, while mostly good, bother me for some issues of unrealistic tone, facts and word choice. No vote for now, pending revision of journal entries.

Edited: to make the language a little clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the slow build of this one though I don't think you do enough with the genocide to really justify invoking it. One quibble in the procedures though, you shouldn't have a d class be in charge of repairing the shell damage. An engineer or robot would make more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is how haunted must be worked. Really nice and as whole, this is a memorable skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. You took that failed "in Soviet Russia, bubbles pop YOU" SCP from a while back and reworked it into something quite good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That song just gave me chills. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. The song makes it for me. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They intended to just destroy the cleansers, but dead spirits or not, they're still a bunch of children, and they probably don't fully understand where they are or what they're doing (much less what effect that has on the outside world).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So did/do the dead intend for the thing to try to destroy the entire world, or was it just intended to destroy the "cleansers"? Or did they not intend anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually considered adding this, but felt it might make things a little too long-winded; the Foundation doesn't know if the fountain is indestructible, but since the fountain is the only thing they have that can contain the liquid inside, they're not about to find out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good. I think one or two of the expungements in the journal are unecessary. Possible to remove those last two and tell us what really happened to all those horrible bastards?

Anyway, that wasn't enough to keep me from enjoying myself, and enjoying the vengeance kind of narrative it painted. Good show.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the fountain indestructible? What would happen with the "liquid" if something or someone destroys or damages the fountain? I think a little more information about the fountain would be nice.

Anyway, I liked this article, specially the song.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not entirely sure. The picture file says limestone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admittedly know nothing about stone, but isn't the depicted fountain made of terra cotta?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
██████████ committed suicide shortly after his initial contact with the Foundation, though his personnel effects were recovered, among which was a journal.

Is this supposed to be personal? It's slightly ambiguous, given that the individual is described as an informant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This works as a Keter and I really like the addition of the song. The addendum before that felt a little long-winded, though the payoff at the end was nice and creepy. One spot that caught me up:

The exposed bubble will reduce in size until it ceases to exist and destroy an amount of solid matter equal to the volume lost (the mass and density of the substance is irrelevant to this reaction).

There's just something about this sentence and/or its position in the paragraph that confused me the first two or three times I read it. No particular suggestion for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if there's any standout moment in this for me, but I really like the overall picture it paints for me. I just get a general "good writing" vibe as I was reading this.
